metadata.title: Guidance for Industry: General Principles for the Development of Vaccines to Protect Against Global Infectious Diseases

rules.rule_text: FDA has authority under section 351 of the PHS Act (42 U.S.C. 262) to license vaccines against diseases that are not endemic or have not been reported to occur in the United States; the regulatory pathway is the same as for vaccines against diseases endemic in the U.S.
rules.verbatim_quote: Section 351 of the PHS Act provides FDA with the authority to approve marketing applications for biological products intended to treat, mitigate, diagnose, or prevent conditions or diseases found in the U.S. as well as diseases or conditions primarily endemic to other countries, or diseases or conditions that have not been reported to occur in the U.S.
rules.exceptions: None; same standards apply regardless of geographic distribution of disease.

rules.rule_text: A BLA for a vaccine must demonstrate that: (a) the vaccine is safe, pure, and potent (safe and effective); and (b) the manufacturing facility complies with current Good Manufacturing Practice (21 CFR 601.2(d)).
rules.verbatim_quote: The requirements for CBER to license a vaccine include a demonstration that: a) the vaccine is safe, pure, and potent (safe and effective); and b) the facility in which the vaccine is manufactured complies with current good manufacturing practice (21 CFR 601.2(d)).
rules.exceptions: None; these are statutory requirements under 42 U.S.C. 262.

rules.rule_text: All regulatory requirements in Title 21, Code of Federal Regulations (21 CFR Parts 600 through 680) apply to all vaccines licensed in the U.S., regardless of their indication or intended target population.
rules.verbatim_quote: The regulatory requirements contained in Title 21, Code of Federal Regulations (21 CFR) apply to all vaccines licensed in the U.S., regardless of their indication or intended target population. These regulations establish the methods and standards for manufacturing a biological product to assure that the product is safe and meets the quality and purity characteristics that it claims to possess (21 CFR Parts 600 through 680).
rules.exceptions: None.

rules.rule_text: Accelerated approval under 21 CFR Part 601, Subpart E may be granted for vaccines for serious or life-threatening illnesses using a surrogate endpoint reasonably likely to predict clinical benefit; postmarketing studies must be conducted to verify clinical benefit.
rules.source_section: III. Applicable Regulations and Legislation / V. FAQ Question 5
rules.verbatim_quote: Accelerated approval may be granted for certain biological products that have been studied for their safety and effectiveness in treating serious or life-threatening illnesses and that provide meaningful therapeutic benefit to patients over existing treatments (21 CFR Part 601, Subpart E).
rules.exceptions: Surrogate endpoint must be reasonably likely to predict clinical benefit; not available for products that do not meet the serious/life-threatening threshold.

rules.rule_text: Under accelerated approval (21 CFR 601.41), approval is based on a surrogate endpoint or a clinical endpoint other than survival or irreversible morbidity that is reasonably likely to predict clinical benefit; postmarketing studies verifying clinical benefit are a condition of approval.
rules.verbatim_quote: Section 601.41 (21 CFR 601.41) sets forth the following requirements for accelerated approval: 1) Approval will be based on adequate and well-controlled clinical trials establishing that the biological product has an effect on a surrogate endpoint that is reasonably likely, based on epidemiologic, therapeutic, pathophysiologic, or other evidence, to predict clinical benefit or on the basis of an effect on a clinical endpoint other than survival or irreversible morbidity.
rules.exceptions: None for the surrogate endpoint requirement.

rules.rule_text: Postmarketing studies for accelerated approval must be adequate, well-controlled, and conducted with due diligence; protocols must be submitted with the original BLA. Marketing approval may be withdrawn if the postmarketing study fails to verify clinical benefit or if the sponsor fails to perform the study with due diligence (21 CFR 601.43(a)(1) and (2)).
rules.verbatim_quote: Marketing approval for biological products approved under 21 CFR 601.41 or 21 CFR 601.42 may be withdrawn, for example, if the postmarketing clinical study fails to verify clinical benefit or the sponsor fails to perform the required postmarketing study with due diligence (21 CFR 601.43(a)(1) and (2)).
rules.exceptions: None.

rules.rule_text: When definitive human efficacy studies are not ethical or feasible, approval may be based on evidence from adequate and well-controlled animal studies under the Animal Rule (21 CFR Part 601, Subpart H); postmarketing field studies verifying clinical benefit must be conducted when ethical and feasible (21 CFR 601.91(b)(1)).
rules.verbatim_quote: In May 2002, FDA published a final rule entitled 'New Drug and Biological Drug Products: Evidence Needed to Demonstrate Effectiveness of New Drug and Biological Products When Human Efficacy Studies Are Not Ethical or Feasible'... This rule applies when definitive human efficacy studies are not ethical or feasible... after approval, a sponsor must conduct post-marketing studies, such as field studies, to verify and describe the biological product's clinical benefit and to assess its safety when used as indicated in circumstances where such studies are feasible and ethical; such post-marketing studies would not be feasible until an exigency arises (21 CFR 601.91(b)(1)).
rules.exceptions: Animal Rule postmarketing studies not required until an exigency arises making field studies feasible.

rules.rule_text: Sponsors developing vaccines against global infectious diseases must submit an IND under 21 CFR Part 312 and are encouraged to begin interactions with CBER early in development through pre-IND meetings.
rules.source_section: IV. Development of Vaccines to Protect Against Global Infectious Disease
rules.verbatim_quote: FDA is encouraging sponsors to develop and license vaccines to protect against global infectious diseases by submitting an IND (21 CFR Part 312), even if the U.S. market for that vaccine may be limited... Sponsors who are interested in developing these vaccines should begin interactions with the Agency early in product development, such as through pre-IND meetings.
rules.exceptions: No user fee for pre-IND and IND submissions or related activities.

rules.rule_text: Prior to initiating Phase 3 clinical trials, sponsors must discuss with CBER the details of study conduct including disease endpoints, study site(s), subject selection, control group choice, trial design parameters, diagnostic tests, dose and dosing schedule, study duration, concomitant vaccinations, and safety assessments.
rules.source_section: IV. Development of Vaccines to Protect Against Global Infectious Disease
rules.verbatim_quote: Thus, prior to initiating Phase 3 clinical trials, we recommend that you discuss with us the details related to study conduct (e.g., issues related to the disease to be prevented or treated, study site(s), subject selection, choice of control group, trial design parameters such as endpoints/case definitions and diagnostic tests, dose and dosing schedule, study duration, concomitant vaccinations and medications, as well as safety assessments) well in advance of study initiation.
rules.exceptions: None.

rules.rule_text: All Phase 3 vaccine trials must be conducted under the provisions of good clinical practice (GCP) in accordance with 21 CFR Parts 312, 50, and 56.
rules.source_section: IV. Development of Vaccines to Protect Against Global Infectious Disease
rules.verbatim_quote: These trials should be conducted under the provisions of good clinical practice (GCP). For FDA regulations relative to GCP and clinical trials, we refer you to the FDA website http://www.fda.gov/oc/gcp/regulations.html.
rules.exceptions: None.

rules.rule_text: Foreign clinical studies conducted under an IND must meet the requirements of 21 CFR Part 312. Foreign studies not conducted under an IND are acceptable to support a BLA only if conducted in accordance with GCP and reviewed and approved by an independent ethics committee (21 CFR 312.120).
rules.verbatim_quote: FDA regulations permit the acceptance of foreign clinical studies in support of a BLA approval, provided certain conditions are met. Foreign studies performed under an IND must meet the requirements of 21 CFR Part 312. Under 21 CFR 312.120, FDA will accept as support for an IND or to support an application for marketing approval a well-designed and well-conducted foreign clinical study not conducted under an IND, if certain conditions are met, including that the study was conducted in accordance with GCP and including review and approval by an independent ethics committee.
rules.exceptions: Non-IND foreign studies require GCP compliance and independent ethics committee review to be acceptable.

rules.rule_text: The impact of ethnic factors on a vaccine's safety and effectiveness must be assessed when relying on foreign clinical data; sponsors should apply the principles in ICH E5 (Ethnic Factors in the Acceptability of Foreign Clinical Data).
rules.verbatim_quote: In addition, it is important to assess the impact of ethnic factors on the vaccine's safety and effectiveness. These principles are discussed in the guidance document entitled, 'E5: Ethnic Factors in the Acceptability of Foreign Clinical Data' dated June 2004 (Ref. 12).
rules.exceptions: None.

rules.rule_text: If pivotal studies supporting licensure are conducted outside the U.S., CBER may request an additional bridging study to assess immunogenicity and/or safety in the U.S. population; sponsors must discuss this need with CBER early in clinical development.
rules.verbatim_quote: If studies to support vaccine licensure are conducted outside the U.S., CBER may request a smaller U.S. study that bridges immunogenicity and/or safety to the U.S. population. The need to conduct such a study will depend on a number of factors and we encourage sponsors to discuss with CBER early in clinical development whether there is a need to conduct a study to bridge immunogenicity and/or safety to the U.S. population.
rules.exceptions: Need for bridging study depends on factors including disease epidemiology, study population, and environmental/medical conditions.

rules.rule_text: Human challenge studies intended to demonstrate either proof of concept or vaccine efficacy must be discussed with CBER prior to initiation; use of challenge studies does not preclude the requirement for adequate safety data.
rules.verbatim_quote: As human challenge studies may present unique considerations, we recommend that the sponsor discuss its development plan with CBER prior to initiation of such studies for either proof of concept or vaccine efficacy.
rules.exceptions: Challenge studies may be used to demonstrate efficacy if adequate, well-controlled, and conducted under GCP.

rules.rule_text: Human challenge studies used to demonstrate efficacy must be adequate, well-controlled, and conducted under GCP; use of challenge studies for efficacy demonstration does not eliminate the need for adequate safety data.
rules.verbatim_quote: Of note, use of challenge studies to demonstrate efficacy does not preclude the requirement for adequate safety data. As human challenge studies may present unique considerations, we recommend that the sponsor discuss its development plan with CBER prior to initiation of such studies.
rules.exceptions: None.

rules.rule_text: Vaccine development generally proceeds stepwise from adults to children; however, for global diseases disproportionately affecting children (e.g., malaria), development may need to start in infants or children.
rules.verbatim_quote: Vaccine development generally takes place in a stepwise fashion from adults to children. However, for many global diseases (e.g., malaria), the pediatric population may face greater mortality or morbidity than the adult population because adults may already be immune. Therefore, it may be appropriate or necessary to start development in infants or children.
rules.exceptions: Scientific justification required if development begins in pediatric population before adults.

rules.rule_text: For pediatric studies in the U.S., institutional review boards must ensure research is compliant with 21 CFR Part 50, Subpart D (additional protections for children).
rules.verbatim_quote: For pediatric studies in the U.S., institutional review boards must ensure research is compliant with 21 CFR Part 50, Subpart D.
rules.exceptions: None.

rules.rule_text: PREA (Pediatric Research Equity Act) requires pediatric assessments to be included in all BLA submissions under section 351 of the PHS Act, unless the sponsor has obtained a waiver or deferral from FDA.
rules.verbatim_quote: The Pediatric Research Equity Act of 2003 (PREA), (Public Law 108-155) as reauthorized in Title IV of FDAAA, addresses product development for pediatric uses. PREA requires pediatric assessments to be included in all applications submitted under section 505 of the FD&C Act or under section 351 of the PHS Act, unless the sponsor has obtained a waiver or deferral from FDA.
rules.exceptions: Waiver or deferral from FDA may be obtained; pediatric effectiveness may be extrapolated from adults if disease and drug effects are sufficiently similar.

rules.rule_text: Sponsors must submit adequate safety information to support pediatric use in the BLA (section 505B(a)(2)(A)(ii) of the FD&C Act); pediatric effectiveness may be extrapolated from adult studies supplemented with immune response data if disease and drug effects are sufficiently similar.
rules.verbatim_quote: If the course of the disease and the effects of the drug are sufficiently similar in adults and pediatric patients, FDA may conclude that pediatric effectiveness can be extrapolated from adequate and well-controlled studies in adults, usually supplemented with other information obtained in pediatric subjects, such as immune response studies (section 505B(a)(2)(B) of the FD&C Act). Sponsors must also submit adequate safety information to support use in the pediatric population (section 505B(a)(2)(A)(ii) of the FD&C Act).
rules.exceptions: Extrapolation requires sufficiently similar disease course and drug effects in adults and pediatric patients.

rules.rule_text: FDA can grant priority review for vaccine applications targeting tropical diseases that disproportionately affect poor and marginalized populations under section 524 of the FD&C Act (21 U.S.C. 360n(a)(3)).
rules.verbatim_quote: Under section 524, the Agency can grant priority review of applications under section 505(b)(1) of the FD&C Act or section 351 of the PHS Act for the treatment and prevention of specified tropical diseases, including tuberculosis, malaria, cholera, and 'any other infectious disease for which there is no significant market in developed nations and that disproportionately affects poor and marginalized populations, designated by regulation by the Secretary.'
rules.exceptions: Priority review is a procedural benefit; it does not modify the evidentiary standard for licensure.

rules.rule_text: The level of evidence necessary to demonstrate safety and effectiveness is the same regardless of whether the disease is endemic to the U.S.; potency has long been interpreted to include efficacy (21 CFR 600.3(s)).
rules.source_section: III. Applicable Regulations and Legislation / V. FAQ Question 4
rules.verbatim_quote: The level of evidence necessary to demonstrate the safety and effectiveness of the vaccine is the same whether or not the disease, for which the vaccine is indicated, is endemic to the U.S... potency has long been interpreted to include efficacy (21 CFR 600.3(s)). Proof of effectiveness generally consists of controlled clinical investigations as defined in the provision for adequate and well-controlled studies for new drugs (See 21 CFR 314.126).
rules.exceptions: Animal Rule and accelerated approval provide alternative pathways but require equivalent rigor.

rules.rule_text: There is no requirement that studies supporting vaccine licensure be conducted in the United States population; CBER evaluates foreign trials taking into account disease epidemiology, study population, and environmental/medical conditions.
rules.verbatim_quote: There is no such requirement. CBER evaluates trials conducted outside the U.S. to determine if the vaccine is safe and effective for use as proposed in labeling. As part of this evaluation, CBER considers factors such as disease epidemiology, the study population, and the environmental and medical care conditions.
rules.exceptions: CBER may still request U.S. bridging study depending on the evaluation factors.

rules.rule_text: Foreign clinical studies not conducted under an IND are accepted to support a BLA only if conducted in accordance with GCP and reviewed and approved by an independent ethics committee (21 CFR 312.120).
rules.verbatim_quote: Under 21 CFR 312.120, FDA will accept as support for an IND or to support an application for marketing approval a well-designed and well-conducted foreign clinical study not conducted under an IND, if certain conditions are met, including that the study was conducted in accordance with GCP and including review and approval by an independent ethics committee.
rules.exceptions: None; both conditions (GCP compliance and ethics committee review) must be met.

rules.rule_text: A BLA user fee is required even if the sponsor does not intend to market the product in the United States; fee waiver may be available for small business entities or sponsors with orphan designation.
rules.verbatim_quote: Yes. The sponsor is required to pay a user fee regardless of whether the sponsor intends to market the licensed product in the U.S. However, sponsors may have the application user fee waived if they meet certain criteria, such as being a small business entity or having an orphan designation for their product.
rules.exceptions: Fee waiver available for small business or orphan-designated products.

rules.rule_text: Vaccines for diseases not endemic in the U.S. are eligible for orphan drug designation if intended for a rare disease or condition based on U.S. disease prevalence; orphan-designated vaccines are still subject to full safety and efficacy evaluation under 21 CFR 601.2(d).
rules.verbatim_quote: A sponsor may apply for designation of its vaccine as an 'orphan drug' if the vaccine is intended for use against a rare disease or condition... please note that a vaccine designated as an orphan product must be evaluated for safety and efficacy like any other vaccine (21 CFR 601.2(d)).
rules.exceptions: Orphan designation provides benefits (no BLA user fee, tax credit, marketing incentives, grant support) but does not alter the evidentiary standard for licensure.

rules.rule_text: Sponsors must submit an IND to receive FDA input on manufacturing, quality testing, assay validation, nonclinical and clinical trial design issues, statistical analysis plans, and endpoints for vaccines against global infectious diseases; the IND process provides scientific and regulatory advice critical to advancement of world health.
rules.verbatim_quote: FDA encourages submission of an IND so that it can provide input on manufacturing, quality testing, assay validation, non-clinical and clinical trial design issues, statistical analysis plans, endpoints, and other important aspects of vaccine development. The IND process will allow sponsors to obtain important scientific and regulatory advice on products that are critical to the advancement of world health.
rules.exceptions: No user fee for pre-IND and IND submissions or related activities (meetings, feedback).
